senses.gloss: the science of grammar as a textual discipline and body of rules
senses.justification: Verses 1.11-15 refer to grammar as a science (vidyā), a discipline, a meta-discipline grounded in textual analysis, and a gateway to the sciences.

senses.gloss: the transformative power or faculty enabling direct access to the absolute
senses.justification: Verses 1.12, 1.18-22, and 1.61 present grammar as a power (śakti) and method (yoga) that provides the direct path and enables realization of Brahman; verse 1.61 refers to the operative power (kāryam) of grammatical rules.

senses.gloss: the imperishable, eternal reality; Brahman itself
senses.justification: Verse 1.9 identifies akṣara as the eternally imperishable that IS purification itself, the syllable through which supreme knowledge comes.

senses.gloss: phonetic signs, syllables, or phonetic elements; sound-letters
senses.justification: Verses 1.18-22 refer to aksara-smrti's signs as the phonetic apparatus—the differentiated sounds (varnas) housed within the highest speech.

senses.gloss: tradition as a source or means of knowledge; the transmitted teaching
senses.justification: Verses 1.27 and 1.30 present āgama as a source from which correct words are established and through which dharma is known—a means of valid knowledge distinct from independent reasoning.

senses.gloss: tradition as a transcendent standing condition; the unbroken continuity presupposed by all reasoning
senses.justification: Verse 1.41 presents āgama as persisting without break (avicchedena) like consciousness itself (caitanyam iva), transcending argumentative reach—a transcendent presupposition rather than a mere means of knowledge.

senses.gloss: inference as a means of knowledge; anumāna as a pramāṇa or instrument
senses.justification: Verses 1.36, 1.38, and 1.42 list anumāna alongside pratyakṣa as one of the conventional pramāṇas (means of knowledge) whose reach the siddhis transcend, or whose limit the highest knowledge exceeds.

senses.gloss: inference as an epistemic faculty or cognitive process
senses.justification: Verses 1.32, 1.35, 1.37 discuss anumāna as an epistemic capacity—establishing things by inference, the claim that anumāna exhausts knowledge, and what the highest knowledge transcends.

senses.gloss: the ultimate principle of righteousness, virtue, and merit; the ethical-ontological ground
senses.justification: Verses 1.24-26, 1.27, 1.29 present dharma as the final aim of grammar, the principle whose realization grammar enables and whose means grammar provides.

senses.gloss: the empirical, transmitted paths and practices of virtue; established customs and traditions
senses.justification: Verses 1.30-31 describe dharma as the unbroken paths established through tradition and world-practice, grounded in empirical continuity rather than reasoning alone.

senses.gloss: the specific relational character or nature; the dharma between ground and manifestation
senses.justification: Verses 1.49, 1.63, 1.114 use dharma to mean the relational character or dharma-connection, referring to the vivarta (manifestation) relation or causal properties where effects appear transferred without ontological change.

senses.gloss: action as the ultimate principle; the basis of cosmic order and ritual efficacy
senses.justification: Verse 1.6 references karma as the absolute ground underlying the manifestation of ritual power and the cosmic order.

senses.gloss: the causal power or force of past actions; karma as the generative power producing attainments
senses.justification: Verse 1.36 states that the siddhis (attainments) of ancestors, raksasas, and pisacas are born of karma—karma as the power that generates supernatural powers.

senses.gloss: the transcendent, inner word as an unchanging principle; the word residing in intellect before utterance; śabdatattva
senses.justification: Verse 1.46 presents śabda as buddhistha (residing in intellect), complete and unsounded, latent as fire in the fire-stick—the absolute sphoṭa that manifests without transformation. Verses 1.94, 1.96, 1.115, 1.116, 1.120, 1.121 continue this usage of śabda as the unchanging word-absolute that persists through manifestation, with manifestation of the many sounds dependent on this transcendent śabda.

senses.gloss: the linguistic term or word as used in utterance, grammar, and communication
senses.justification: Verses across Brahmakāṇḍa refer to śabda as the linguistic item operating in communication and grammar, including the undivided word in various manifestations and contexts.

senses.gloss: the audible utterance or phonetic manifestation of sound; the many differentiated sounds
senses.justification: Verses 1.107, 1.110-111 refer to śabda as the manifested, audible sound that appears in the empirical world, contrasted with the underlying word-principle.

senses.gloss: semantic power; the force by which words signify and mean
senses.justification: Verse 1.23 discusses the eternal relation between word and meaning, treating artha as the inherent power (śakti) of words to convey significance.

senses.gloss: meaning as a property conveyed or as the attribute of meaningfulness
senses.justification: Verses 1.27, 1.44, 1.47, 1.62, and 1.67 establish that words succeed in conveying meaning or in standing for referents, treating artha as a property they possess or the relational character that justifies derivation.

senses.gloss: purpose, aim, or ultimate goal; that for which all activity is undertaken
senses.justification: Verse 1.29 invokes purpose (artha): no one establishes a purposeless system, so grammar's codification must serve an ultimate artha (here identified with dharma).

senses.gloss: a matter, subject, or object of knowledge; the thing being discussed or inferred
senses.justification: Verse 1.34 refers to a matter inferred with great effort, treating artha as the subject-matter of inference that remains vulnerable to reversal.

senses.gloss: the manifested referent; the object as revealed or manifested through word and cognition
senses.justification: Verses 1.50, 1.53-57, 1.61, 1.74, 1.119 present artha as the manifested object (jneya), the thing that is known and expressed—artha as the empirical manifestation revealed through the speaker and hearer's understanding, and as the undivided meaning that language conveys.

senses.gloss: ultimate knowledge of purification and realization
senses.justification: Verse 1.9 identifies vidyā as the true purification itself, the ultimate knowledge reached through the syllable Om that contradicts no doctrine.

senses.gloss: the empirical sciences and disciplined bodies of knowledge
senses.justification: Verses 1.15, 1.125 refer to 'the sciences' (vidyā) as empirical fields of knowledge of which grammar is the final resort and culmination, or as sciences known through the word.

senses.gloss: perception as a means of knowledge; pratyakṣa as a pramāṇa or instrument
senses.justification: Verses 1.36 and 1.42 list pratyakṣa alongside anumāna as one of the conventional pramāṇas (means of knowledge) whose reach the siddhis transcend or whose limit the highest knowledge exceeds.

senses.gloss: perception as an epistemic faculty or immediate knowledge
senses.justification: Verses 1.37 and 1.39 describe the highest knowledge as having perception's immediacy (aparokṣa), not distinguishable from perception—pratyakṣa as the character of direct epistemic awareness.

senses.gloss: traditional transmitted texts and knowledge; the body of secondary literature derived from the Veda
senses.justification: Verses 1.7, 1.29, and 1.43 refer to smṛti as the traditional texts framed by vedavid (those who know the Veda), transmitted without interruption, providing the grounds for the regulation of words.

senses.gloss: the meaning-bearing linguistic universal; the word-whole that resides in intellect and manifests through utterance
senses.justification: Verses 1.44, 1.45, 1.49, 1.53, 1.70, 1.75, 1.76, 1.77 present sphoṭa as the unified meaning-bearer distinguished from the serial audible sounds (dhvani); it is the non-sequential linguistic principle that remains constant across utterances.

senses.gloss: the first-produced acoustic sound; the initial sound generated by articulation (phonetician usage, distinct from Bhartṛhari's doctrine)
senses.justification: Verses 1.102, 1.107 report an alternative terminology from phonetician-grammarians. The sphoṭa here is acoustic and produced (not metaphysical), described as 'the one generated by the articulators' contacts and releases,' making it a manifestation of the causal power of articulation. Verse 1.106 explicitly references '1.102's usage.' These verses are positioned within a tour (1.102–113) of non-Bhartṛhari theories about sound and meaning.

senses.gloss: a grammatical constituent; a dependent member in a compound or larger unit
senses.justification: Verse 1.62 presents guṇa as a dependent linguistic element (constituent inside a compound or bare stem), explicitly contrasting it with metaphysical quality.

senses.gloss: a quality or attribute; the ground of pre-eminence or distinction
senses.justification: Verse 1.64 uses guṇa to mean a quality that is the prakarsa-hetu (ground of pre-eminence), resting on its substrate and enabling distinction.

senses.gloss: time as empirical circumstance conditioning causal powers
senses.justification: 1.32 lists time among the circumstances (state, place, time) that index powers

senses.gloss: duration/vowel-quantity as a property of the manifesting sound
senses.justification: 1.76: the kala of the primary sound (short, long, prolated) is figuratively attributed to the word

senses.gloss: the audible sounds distinguished from the underlying word-principle; acoustic phonetic manifestations
senses.justification: Verses 1.44, 1.47, 1.49, 1.71, 1.75, 1.78, 1.80, 1.81, 1.83, 1.84, 1.92, 1.93, 1.97, 1.104, 1.106 present dhvani as the linguistic item of audible sounds, distinct from sphoṭa (the word-principle). Verses 1.104, 1.106 occur within the phonetician-grammarian framework (1.102–113) but treat dhvani as the manifestable linguistic element.

senses.gloss: the sounds as empirical acoustic phenomena; manifested audible forms (phonetician framework)
senses.justification: Verses 1.94, 1.96, 1.102 present dhvani as the manifested sounds—the acoustic phenomena generated by articulation. Verse 1.102 occurs within the phonetician-grammarian framework reporting that dhvani are 'sounds born from sphota' (the first-produced acoustic sound), making them manifestations of acoustic causation.

senses.gloss: being, existence, or being-ness as the absolute principle
senses.justification: Verse 1.100 presents bhāva as the ultimate being or existence-principle that underlies manifestation.

senses.gloss: becoming, manifestation, or empirical existence; the actualization of being
senses.justification: Verse 1.38 uses bhāva to mean the manifested, actualized state or becoming, contrasted with the unmanifest potential.

senses.gloss: the audible sound as phenomenal manifestation; acoustic vibrations
senses.justification: Verses 1.48, 1.101 refer to nāda as the sound as it appears in experience, the acoustic manifestation in the medium (like ripples in water or vibrations in air).

senses.gloss: sound as a linguistic or structural element; coordinated with articulation
senses.justification: Verse 1.105 treats nāda as a linguistic element within the phonetician framework, referring to 'bare sound' (dhvani-matram) reaching the ear—sound as a structured element of utterance.

senses.gloss: the articulators or organs of speech as productive power; instrumental cause
senses.justification: Verse 1.102 refers to karaṇa as the organs (samyoga-vibhaga, contacts and releases) that generate the sphota—the articulators as the instrumental cause of sound production in the phonetician framework.

senses.gloss: means, instrument, or the relational character of instrumentality
senses.justification: Verse 1.47 uses karaṇa to denote the instrument or means by which an effect is produced—the relational property of being instrumental.

senses.gloss: the articulators as linguistic or grammatical elements; articulation as a phonetic category
senses.justification: Verse 1.106 refers to karaṇa (articulation) as a linguistic process—'while the karaṇa still trembles, the articulation not yet settled'—treating it as a structural element within phonetic generation.

senses.gloss: the absolute principle of distinctiveness; the differentiating principle of Brahman
senses.justification: Verse 1.33 refers to viśeṣa as an absolute principle that characterizes Brahman—the distinctiveness that makes transcendent reality determinate.

senses.gloss: distinction, particularity, or a specific differentiating characteristic
senses.justification: Verse 1.105 uses viśeṣa to denote specific differences or particularities—distinctions that separate phenomena (like differences in sound quality).

senses.gloss: the sign, mark, or indicator; a linguistic mark or indicator of meaning
senses.justification: Verse 1.18-22 refers to nimitta in the context of aksara-smrti signs as phonetic marks or indicators of the underlying word-principle.

senses.gloss: the instrumental cause or efficient condition; the conditioning power
senses.justification: Verses 1.94, 1.98 refer to nimitta as the condition or efficient cause of manifestation—'being the condition of apprehension' (1.94) and the grounds on which manifestation depends.

senses.gloss: accumulation, aggregation, or the massing of similar elements
senses.justification: Verse 1.105 refers to pracaya as the accumulated, manifested plurality—many things massed together or accumulated through successive manifestations.

senses.gloss: the relational property of accumulation or aggregative connection
senses.justification: Verse 1.108-109 uses pracaya to denote the property or character of accumulation—how successive manifestations connect and build up through the causal chain.

senses.gloss: the wind or air as a causal power; the medium or vehicle of transmission
senses.justification: Verses 1.107, 1.114 refer to vāyu as the instrumental power (the wind/air) through which sound propagates and manifests—the causal force enabling manifestation.

senses.gloss: the air or wind as the empirical medium; atmospheric phenomena
senses.justification: Verse 1.108-109 refers to vāyu as the manifested, empirical air or wind—the phenomenon through which sound waves propagate and become perceptible.

senses.gloss: seeing, perception, or direct vision; the epistemic faculty of sight
senses.justification: Verses 1.18-22, 1.74, 1.89 refer to darśana as direct perception or seeing—an epistemic faculty or mode of knowing through visual or intuitive apprehension.

senses.gloss: sight or viewing as a relational property; the capacity or property of being visible
senses.justification: Verse 1.107 refers to darśana as the property of being visible or perceptible—the relational character that allows something to be seen or known.

senses.gloss: effort, exertion, or the relational character of instrumentality in causing effort
senses.justification: Verse 1.108-109 refers to prayatna as the effort or striving property that characterizes the instrument or means of causation.

senses.gloss: effort as a causal power; the productive force of endeavor or instrumental exertion
senses.justification: Verse 1.110-111 presents prayatna as a power—the generative force of effort through which manifestations occur (like effort in breathing producing phonetic forms).

senses.gloss: the breath as a vital, causal power; the generative force underlying manifestation
senses.justification: Verse 1.115 presents prāṇa as the power of breath—'the breath ascends carrying the undivided word'—as the instrumental power through which the word-principle manifests into differentiated forms.

senses.gloss: the breath as a manifested physiological phenomenon; the empirical life-force
senses.justification: Verses 1.112-113, 1.117 refer to prāṇa as the manifested breath or life-force—the empirical phenomenon through which utterance and embodied existence appear.

senses.gloss: intellect or intelligence as the epistemic faculty; the faculty of understanding and discrimination
senses.justification: Verses 1.45, 1.46, 1.47, 1.53, 1.70, 1.84, 1.90, 1.91 refer to buddhi as the intellectual faculty where the word resides, through which meaning is grasped, and by which discrimination and knowledge operate.

senses.gloss: the manifested intellect; the actualized cognitive state or intellectual phenomenon
senses.justification: Verse 1.117 refers to buddhi as the manifested, actualized state of intelligence—the empirical phenomenon of cognition appearing within the embodied utterance.

senses.gloss: difference, distinction, or separation as an empirical phenomenon; manifested multiplicity
senses.justification: Verses 1.6, 1.18-22, 1.86, 1.117, 1.118 refer to bheda as visible or manifested differences—the many appearing as distinct, like different forms of dharma or different sounds born from a single source.

senses.gloss: the relational property of differentiation; the character or nature of being different
senses.justification: Verses 1.99, 1.119 use bheda to denote the specific relational property or character of differentiation—the nature of how things differ or are distinguished.

senses.gloss: speech, utterance, or the manifested word as spoken sound
senses.justification: Verses 1.86, 1.122 refer to vāk as speech or utterance—the manifested, audible word appearing in the world through phonetic forms.

senses.gloss: speech or the word as a transcendent principle; the absolute, unmeasured word-principle
senses.justification: Verse 1.125 refers to vāk as the transcendent word—'the unmeasured speech from which knowing is born'—treated as an absolute principle underlying all manifestation.

senses.gloss: illumination or revelation as a causal power; the power by which things become manifest
senses.justification: Verse 1.37 presents prakāśa as the revealing power—the force through which hidden things are made known or manifest.

senses.gloss: the ultimate principle of illumination or light; consciousness as absolute luminosity
senses.justification: Verse 1.94 refers to prakāśa as the absolute light or illumination—'the unchanging word manifests the many sounds by its prakāśa' (illuminating power), treating prakāśa as the absolute principle of manifestation.

senses.gloss: manifestation in consciousness; the appearing of phenomena to awareness
senses.justification: Verse 1.124 uses prakāśa to mean manifestation in consciousness—'the word manifests as prakāśa in the intellect,' treating prakāśa as the mode of conscious appearance.

senses.gloss: a name, designation, or conventional linguistic sign
senses.justification: Verse 1.66 refers to samjñā as a name or designation—a linguistic item used conventionally to mark or identify something.

senses.gloss: recognition, identification, or the cognitive act of naming and knowing
senses.justification: Verse 1.126 uses samjñā to mean the cognitive act of recognition or knowing through designation—'the child knows by samjñā,' the identification and naming through which cognition operates.

senses.gloss: class, genus, or generative principle; the power of class-nature
senses.justification: Verses 1.68-69, 1.93, 1.95 refer to jāti as the generative class-nature or power—the principle by which particulars share a common nature and causally produce effects.

senses.gloss: caste, status, or the relational property of classification
senses.justification: Verse 1.126 uses jāti to denote the property or status of belonging to a class—the relational characteristic that distinguishes one group from another.

senses.gloss: place, location, or space as an empirical circumstance
senses.justification: Verse 1.32 lists place (deśa) among the empirical circumstances that index or condition powers.

senses.gloss: spatial position or the relational property of location
senses.justification: Verse 1.96 refers to deśa as the spatial property or relational character—'the sounds are many and located'—the property by which things are positioned in space.

senses.gloss: fragrance or odor as a sensory property; the quality of smell
senses.justification: Verse 1.79 refers to gandha as a property or quality—one of the sensory properties like color, taste, touch that characterize manifestations.

senses.gloss: smell or scent as a manifested sensory phenomenon; olfactory experience
senses.justification: Verse 1.98 refers to gandha as the manifested smell or scent—the empirical sensory phenomenon through which fragrance appears in experience.

senses.gloss: the causal power or potency; the refining, perfecting, preparing power that renders things capable
senses.justification: Verses 1.10, 1.78, 1.80, 1.87 refer to saṃskāra as the power of refinement or preparation—a causal force that prepares or conditions both objects and organs for further effects. Per 1.80 precedent: 'the preparation (saṃskāra) of both — object and organ — is accepted' as a power.

senses.gloss: impressions, residues, or traces laid down in consciousness; the manifestation of prior conditioning
senses.justification: Verse 1.121 uses saṃskāra to mean word-formed residues or impressions—'the child comprehends through residues laid down before'—the manifested traces through which prior conditioning appears and operates.
